text: Characterizing the biomolecular interface between nanocarriers and biological fluids is essential for predicting their physiological behavior. Here, we present a complementary analysis of protein adsorption onto hydroxyethyl starch (HES) nanocapsules, distinguishing between the "hard" and "soft" protein corona using techniques that operate both with and without the extraction of particles from the medium. While high-abundance proteins like Human Serum Albumin (HSA) were notably absent from the hard corona—as determined by SDS-PAGE and protein quantification assays—in situ analysis using Isothermal Titration Calorimetry (ITC) and Dynamic Light Scattering (DLS) revealed a different reality. These methods detected a significant soft corona characterized by high-stoichiometry, low-affinity interactions, effectively identifying HSA as a major soft corona component. Conversely, Apolipoprotein A-I (ApoA-I) exhibited high binding affinities and was identified as a stable constituent of the hard corona. Our findings highlight how traditional washing and centrifugation steps can fundamentally alter the perceived biological identity of a nanoparticle. We conclude that a dual-experimental approach is necessary to capture the full complexity of the protein corona, as the soft corona may play a more significant role in vivo than common extraction-based methods suggest.
